summary: Slovenia's posture is led by the Government Information Security Office (URSIV), which warns that post-quantum cryptography is becoming a necessary part of cybersecurity because today's cryptographic solutions will not withstand quantum computers. URSIV has published a recommendation explaining the European Union's roadmap for post-quantum cryptography and has run training for information security leaders in critical infrastructure and public administration covering post-quantum cryptography and NIS2 obligations. No separate fixed national migration deadline has been published.
govActors: URSIV | Slovenia's Government Information Security Office, issues guidance and recommendations on post-quantum cryptography

EU-aligned by membership: participates in the NIS Cooperation Group coordinated PQC roadmap (high-risk by 2030, full by 2035); the timeline shown is the EU coordinated roadmap, not a national one.
Model 2026-06-14 (EU / taker, High): EU coordinated roadmap (NIS Cooperation Group); adopts the NIST baseline as a taker. No national algorithm.

mainRegulation:
NIS2 Directive (EU) 2022/2555, Art. 21(2)(h) | EU | binding-law
DORA, Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 (financial sector) | EU | binding-law
Commission Recommendation (EU) 2024/1101 on a coordinated PQC roadmap | EU | soft-law
NIS Cooperation Group Coordinated Implementation Roadmap (2026/2030/2035) | EU | soft-law
obligation: Essential and important entities must use state-of-the-art cryptography under NIS2, transposed into national law, and financial entities face equivalent duties under DORA; neither yet names post-quantum algorithms specifically. Migration follows the EU coordinated roadmap, with high-risk use cases targeted for 2030 and full migration by 2035, which is encouraged rather than mandated.
